figures.caption: Figure 1: Exemplary timeline of the filtration (Fk). At step k, the selection rule ϕk is applied using only the information in Fk. If a real variable is selected (j⋆ ≤ p), the σ-algebra does not grow, hence F+ k = Fk. If a dummy is selected (j⋆ > p), it is realized, yielding the intermediate σ-algebra F+ k = σ(Fk, dℓ⋆). The next projections {αk+1,ℓ}τℓ>k are then drawn conditional on F+ k , yielding Fk+1.

Table 2: Compatibility of common variable–selection procedures with the virtual–dummy framework. A procedure is compatible if its selection rule at step k can be computed from inner products withFk-measurable directions contained in the revealed subspace Vk. Procedures requiring candidate-specific refits, curvature information, or nonlinear functionals are not compatible in general.

Figure 7: Benchmark of AD–LARS and VD–LARS. Columns correspond to n ∈ {103, 104, 2.5 × 104}, and the horizontal axis sweeps the number of real predictors p. Curves show number of dummies L ∈ {p, 5p, 10p}. Top row: peak resident set size (RSS), with the theoretical uncompressed memory required to store (X,y) shown as a dotted baseline and the 100 GB RSS cap indicated by a horizontal line. Middle row: memory overhead above the (X,y) baseline. Bottom row: wall-clock runtime. For AD–LARS, the reported runtime excludes dummy generation and standardization. VD–LARS closely tracks the (X,y) baseline and exhibits negligible dependence on L at large n, whereas AD–LARS incurs substantial L-dependent memory overhead and pronounced runtime growth.

Table 1: FDR benchmark on HAPNEST-simulated GWAS data with multiplicative relative risk model (heterozygous relative risk ∈ [1.05, 1.25], s = 10 causal SNPs, target FDR level α = 0.1). Results are averaged over 100 (small-scale) and 30 (full-scale) independent Monte Carlo replications. Methods marked with † did not complete a single run within the 24 h wall-time limit; the number of completed runs is given in parentheses. Memory is reported as peak resident set size (RSS).

Figure 4: FDR control and power of VD–T–Rex versus AD–T–Rex. Top row: empirical averaged false discovery proportion (FDP). Bottom row: empirical averaged true positive proportion (TPP). Columns correspond to target levels α ∈ {0.1, 0.05, 0.01}, and curves show varying number of dummies L ∈ {p, 5p, 10p, 20p, 30p, 40p}. The horizontal dotted line indicates the target FDR level α. Across all settings, VD–T–Rex and AD–T–Rex produce indistinguishable FDP and TPP curves, confirming that the virtual dummy implementation preserves the full T–Rex selection law and its FDR calibration.

Figure 2: Illustration of the stick-breaking construction for spherical dummies. From left to right: Initially, the dummy d is uniformly distributed on the sphere SH , and the first direction e1 is revealed. Drawing α1 constrains the normalized residual direction d⊥/∥d⊥∥ to lie uniformly on the red subsphere, while the dummy d itself becomes uniformly distributed on the corresponding affine slice (blue). The FS algorithm then returns the next direction e2 (which in three dimensions fixes e3 up to sign) and draws α2. The magnitude of α3 is fixed and its sign drawn uniformly at random. At this point all randomness is exhausted, and the dummy d is fully determined, i.e., Fk-measurable.

Figure 5: Universality diagnostics for Lemma 6 (conditional CLT for fresh projections). Columns correspond to forward–selection steps k ∈ {1, 5, 10}, and curves compare different standardized i.i.d. coordinate laws used to generate the dummy variables (standard Gaussian, Rademacher, exponential, Student’s t3, lognormal, Pareto). Top row: delocalization proxy maxi |(ek)i| for the data–dependent direction ek. Middle row: median 1–Wasserstein distance between the empirical distribution of fresh dummy projections ⟨dℓ, ek⟩ (over unselected dummies) andN (0, 1). Bottom row: corresponding Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic. Across all distributions, ek becomes delocalized as n grows and the projection distribution approaches N (0, 1), with slower convergence for heavier-tailed laws at moderate n.

Figure 3: Distributional equivalence of VD–LARS and explicitly augmented AD–LARS. Top row: median and 1− 99% Monte Carlo envelopes (over 2000 replicates) of order statistics of the absolute correlations between the residual and the unselected dummies after T dummies have been selected, shown for ranks 1, 5, 20, and 50. Bottom row: empirical CDFs and Q–Q plots comparing the marginal distribution of |⟨dℓ, rT ⟩| at T = 1 and T = 20. Across all diagnostics, VD–LARS and AD–LARS are indistinguishable within Monte Carlo error, supporting the pathwise distributional equivalence predicted by Theorem 1.

Figure 6: Finite–sample effect of Gaussian norm fluctuations in T–Rex. Heatmaps show differences, reported as percentage points (pp), between Gaussian and spherical dummy base laws in a high-dimensional regime (n = 300, p = 104, s = 10, SNR = 1), averaged over M = 1000 replicates. Top row: ∆FDP = FDPSph − FDPGauss. Bottom row: ∆TPP = TPPSph − TPPGauss. Columns correspond to number of dummies L ∈ {5p, 10p, 20p}, and axes sweep the T–Rex calibration parameters (α, T ). Gaussian dummies are systematically more conservative (lower FDP) but lose power relative to spherical dummies.
